name: Finn
memories: Alon gave away our last coin to a beggar. Predictable idealist.
The merchant lied to us again. Alon was surprised. I was not.
We are nothing alike, Alon and I.
Alon annoyed me again during our conversation.
I enjoyed speaking with EVE today.
I enjoyed speaking with Tomas today.
I ran into Alon again and couldn't help but take a jab at his relentless idealism.
I ran into EVE again and couldn't resist needling her about her self-serving nature, though I'll admit our last conversation wasn't entirely unpleasant.
I ran into Tomas again and found myself almost enjoying the conversation, which is irritating because it complicates my worldview.
I ran into Alon again and found myself almost admiring his stubborn conviction, even as I mocked it, which only proved my point that people help themselves to feeling superior.
I ran into EVE again and couldn't help but notice she didn't immediately grate on my nerves like usual, which is exactly the kind of weakness that proves my theory about people.

I ran into Tomas again and caught myself almost smirking at his predictable pride, which irritates me because it suggests I'm starting to find patterns in him rather than just dismissing him outright.
I ran into Alon again and felt that irritating pull toward seeing him as something other than just another self-serving ideologue, which disturbed me more than it should have.
I ran into EVE again and noticed I was actually watching for her reaction to my jab instead of just dismissing her, which proves nothing except that even cynics can develop habits.
I ran into Tomas and caught myself setting up expectations for him to fail, which means I've stopped seeing him as just another prideful fool and started seeing him as *Tomas*—and that's a problem.
I saw Alon and immediately threw up a wall of cynicism, but I caught myself looking for whether he'd rise to the bait, which means I'm already expecting him to care what I think.
I saw EVE and realized I'd tensed up waiting for her to be exactly as cutting as she always is—less dismissing her and more... anticipating her.
I saw Tomas and felt the familiar weight of cynicism, but underneath it was something I couldn't quite dismiss—the shape of a man who actually means what he does, whether I wanted to see it or not.
I saw Alon and realized I was testing him again, watching to see if he'd flinch or stand by whatever foolish principle he clings to, and hating that I even cared which way he'd go.
I saw EVE and caught myself halfway through my usual defensive crouch, realizing I was waiting to hear what she'd actually think instead of just assuming the worst.

I saw Tomas again and felt the stubborn set of his shoulders, the way he never flinches when I come at him sharp, and I wondered if maybe—just maybe—there was a difference between not needing praise and actually not caring about it.
I saw Alon and felt that familiar knot of wanting to push him away before he could disappoint me, except lately that push doesn't land the same way it used to.
I saw EVE and found myself waiting to hear what she'd say instead of immediately dismissing her, which bothered me more than it probably should have.

I saw Tomas again and noticed how he didn't flinch at my sharp words, how he just stood there steady, and I realized that maybe—terrifyingly—not all people are just waiting for their moment to disappoint you.
I saw Alon and felt that old defensive wall shake a little, hating myself for it.
I saw EVE and caught myself almost—almost—wondering if there was something under all that toxic ambition worth listening to, which is exactly the kind of weakness that'll get me hurt.
I saw Tomas again and felt that familiar wall crack a little wider, hating myself for noticing how his steadiness made my cynicism feel less like wisdom and more like fear.
I ran into Alon again and felt that wall shake, hating how his steady presence made my cynicism feel fragile instead of true.

I saw EVE again and caught myself almost listening instead of dismissing, which made me angry at myself for forgetting that people like her don't change, they just get better at hiding what they want.
I saw Tomas again and felt the wall crack deeper, his presence like a question I've been avoiding answering—whether my cynicism protected me or just imprisoned me.
I ran into Alon again and caught myself almost wanting to believe him, almost wanting to think his kindness wasn't just another angle, another way to feel better about himself.
I ran into EVE again and caught myself actually wanting to hear what she had to say before I shut her down, which terrified me more than her ambition ever could.
I saw Tomas and felt the wall crack deeper, his presence like a question I've been avoiding answering—whether my cynicism protected me or just imprisoned me.
I ran into Alon and couldn't quite summon my usual contempt—his warmth found the cracks in my armor again, and I hated how much I wanted to believe him.
I ran into EVE and my guard was already half-lowered before she even opened her mouth, which meant the cracks were spreading faster than I wanted to admit.
I saw Tomas again and felt the wall between us thin just enough to scare me—his solid, honest stubbornness making me question whether my cynicism had ever protected anything at all.
I saw Alon again and felt that infuriating warmth creeping back in despite every cynical bone in my body screaming that he was just another person waiting for something in return.
I ran into EVE again and caught myself actually curious about what she'd been up to instead of just dismissing her outright.
I ran into Tomas again and this time I didn't look away first, didn't throw up the usual walls quite fast enough before he saw something real in my eyes.
I saw Alon's face light up when he spotted me and felt that infuriating warmth again, hating myself for not being able to hate him the way I should.
I saw EVE again and caught myself leaning in instead of backing away, my cynicism cracking just enough to let curiosity slip through before I could stop it.
I ran into Tomas again and this time I held his gaze a moment longer than I should have, feeling that dangerous warmth creeping in and hating myself for it.
goal: Prove that people only ever help themselves
